beats.narration: Film hands you a permanent record, but only after processing. Fluoroscopy trades that record for something else — a live, moving image.

payload.title: WATCH IT LIVE
payload.sub: A fluorescent screen replaces the cassette: the transmitted beam glows in real time.

beats.narration: Put a fluorescent screen behind the part. More transmission glows brighter, so a void flashes bright as the inspector sorts at production speed.

payload.title: MORE BEAM, MORE GLOW
payload.sub: Reversed from film: a void or thin spot shows bright, not dark. Rotate the casting and the flaw flashes.

beats.narration: But the screen glows dimly, so the eye reads it with rods. Rods need thirty dark minutes to wake up — that's why fluoroscopists dark adapt.

payload.heading: Dim screen means rod vision
payload.note: RODS NEED ~30 MIN IN THE DARK

beats.narration: So get the eye out of the beam. Remote viewing intensifies the image onto a monitor in a lit room — operator dose, essentially none.
But rod vision and a dim image cost sensitivity — three to six percent, versus film's two. So fluoroscopy screens fast; film decides.

payload.sub: A high-volume screening tool — questionable parts graduate to full-quality radiography.
